section.title: Elijah and the Widow
section.sectionRationale: Chapter 16 ended with Ahab enthroning Baal in Samaria. Chapter 17 opens abruptly with Elijah, a prophet who appears from nowhere, announcing a drought that will silence Baal's supposed rule over storm and fertility until the LORD's word releases it. Fed first by ravens at a hidden brook and then, when that fails, by a poor Sidonian widow's never emptying jar and jug, and finally raising her son from death, Elijah is shown again and again to be sustained and vindicated only by the word of the LORD.
section.thesis: In the very region associated with Baal's supposed dominion over storm and harvest, the LORD alone controls the rain, and He proves it by sustaining His prophet and a foreign widow's household through famine and by giving back her dead son's breath, so that both Elijah and the widow come to know that the word of the LORD in Elijah's mouth is truth.
section.frameNote: Four beats: Elijah's oath and the ravens at Cherith, verses 1 to 7; the widow's last handful of flour and oil that do not run out, verses 8 to 16; the death of her son, verses 17 to 18; and Elijah's prayer and the boy's life restored, verses 19 to 24.

verses.text: Now Elijah the Tishbite, who was among the settlers of Gilead, said to Ahab, “As surely as the LORD, the God of Israel, lives, before whom I stand, there will be neither dew nor {{matar|rain}} in these years except at my word!”
Then a revelation from the LORD came to Elijah:
“Leave here, turn eastward, and hide yourself by the Brook of Cherith, east of the Jordan.
And you are to drink from the brook, and I have commanded the ravens to feed you there.”
So Elijah did what the LORD had told him, and he went and lived by the Brook of Cherith, east of the Jordan.
The ravens would bring him bread and meat in the morning and evening, and he would drink from the brook.
Some time later, however, the brook dried up because there had been no rain in the land.

units.label: The widow's last handful
units.note: flour and oil that do not run out

verses.text: Then the word of the LORD came to Elijah:
“Get up and go to Zarephath of Sidon, and stay there. Behold, I have commanded a widow there to provide for you.”

So Elijah got up and went to Zarephath. When he arrived at the city gate, there was a widow gathering sticks. Elijah called to her and said, “Please bring me a little water in a cup, so that I may drink.”
And as she was going to get it, he called to her and said, “Please bring me a piece of {{lechem|bread}}.”

But she replied, “As surely as the LORD your God lives, I have no bread, only a handful of flour in a jar and a little {{shemen|oil}} in a jug. Look, I am gathering a couple of sticks to take home and prepare a meal for myself and my son, so that we may eat it and die.”

“Do not be afraid,” Elijah said to her. “Go and do as you have said. But first make me a small cake of bread from what you have, and bring it out to me. Afterward, make some for yourself and your son,
for this is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: ‘The jar of flour will not be exhausted and the jug of oil will not run dry until the day the LORD sends rain upon the face of the earth.’”
So she went and did according to the word of Elijah, and there was food every day for Elijah and the woman and her household.
The jar of flour was not exhausted and the jug of oil did not run dry, according to the word that the LORD had spoken through Elijah.

units.label: Death enters the house
units.note: what have you done to me

verses.text: Later, the son of the woman who owned the house became ill, and his sickness grew worse and worse, until no breath remained in him.
“O {{ishElohim|man of God}},” said the woman to Elijah, “what have you done to me? Have you come to remind me of my iniquity and cause the death of my son?”

units.label: Elijah prays, and the boy lives
units.note: the word of the LORD is truth

verses.text: But Elijah said to her, “Give me your son.” So he took him from her arms, carried him to the upper room where he was staying, and laid him on his own bed.
Then he cried out to the LORD, “O LORD my God, have You also brought tragedy on this widow who has opened her home to me, by causing her son to die?”
Then he stretched himself out over the child three times and cried out to the LORD, “O LORD my God, please let this boy’s life return to him!”
And the LORD listened to the voice of Elijah, and the child’s life returned to him, and he lived.
Then Elijah took the child, brought him down from the upper room into the house, and gave him to his mother. “Look, your son is alive,” Elijah declared.
Then the woman said to Elijah, “Now I know that you are a man of God and that the word of the LORD from your mouth is truth.”

matar.gloss: Rain. Elijah's oath in verse 1, sworn in the LORD's own name, shuts up matar over the land for years; the chapter's central sign of God's favor and provision, later withheld and restored, is rain itself, showing that the LORD, not Baal, the Canaanite storm god, controls the skies.

lechem.gloss: Bread, food. The widow's request is for a piece of lechem when she has almost none left; the LORD's provision through Elijah is precisely of this ordinary staple, multiplied day by day rather than given all at once, teaching daily dependence on Him.

shemen.gloss: Oil, usually olive oil, a staple used for cooking, light, and anointing. The widow's jug of shemen, paired with her jar of flour, becomes the vessel of an ongoing miracle that does not run dry until the drought itself ends, a small household sign of the same LORD who withholds and gives rain.

ishElohim.gloss: Man of God, a title given to true prophets who speak and act with the LORD's own authority. The widow calls Elijah this in her grief, verse 18, and again in verse 24 confesses it as settled conviction once her son is raised: the word of the LORD in his mouth is truth.

basic.context: Ahab has just enthroned Baal in Samaria. Elijah appears without introduction and announces, in the LORD's name, that there will be no rain except at his word, a direct challenge to Baal's claimed control over storm and harvest. The LORD hides and feeds Elijah by ravens at a brook, then, when the brook dries up, sends him to a poor widow in Sidon, Jezebel's own homeland, whose last handful of flour and oil miraculously do not run out. When her son later dies, Elijah prays urgently and the LORD restores the boy's life. The chapter raises the question of why the LORD would provide for His prophet through a foreign widow rather than within Israel, and what it means that even death yields to the LORD's word.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative recounting real, unrepeatable miracles; it is not a formula guaranteeing a never emptying pantry or an automatic resurrection for every act of faith. What endures as timeless truth is the LORD's provision for those who trust His word, His hearing of urgent, even anguished prayer, and His freedom to extend mercy to whomever He chooses, including those outside the covenant people.
basic.originalLanguages: Elijah's oath concerns matar, rain, silencing the very thing Baal claimed to control. The widow's need is for lechem, bread, and her supply of shemen, oil, is what the LORD promises will not run dry. When her son dies, she calls Elijah ish ha-elohim, man of God, first in accusation and then, once the boy lives again, in full confession that the word of the LORD in his mouth is truth.
basic.typology: Elijah's raising of the widow's son by urgent, repeated prayer anticipates a greater raising still to come. Later Elisha will raise the Shunammite's son in a strikingly similar way, and Jesus Himself will one day raise the dead simply by His own word, needing to plead with no one.
basic.christ: Jesus points directly to this widow in Luke 4:25-26 to show that God's saving grace was never confined to physical Israel, foreshadowing His own rejection at Nazareth and the gospel's reach to the nations. The bread and oil that never run out anticipate Christ, the bread of life, whose provision for His people never fails, John 6:35. And Elijah's prayer that returns breath to a dead child points forward to Christ, who raises the dead by His own authority and word, and who was Himself raised as the firstfruits of the resurrection.
basic.god: God is sovereign over nature, shutting and opening the heavens at His word. He is a compassionate provider who sustains the humanly hopeless, a poor widow down to her last meal, and He hears urgent, even wrestling prayer. He keeps His promises with striking precision, and He is free to show mercy to an outsider even while judging Israel's idolatry.
basic.crossReferences: Luke 4:25-26 has Jesus naming this very widow to show God's mercy reaching beyond Israel. James 5:17-18 holds up Elijah's prayer as a pattern for ordinary, fervent prayer. Second Kings 4 tells of Elisha raising the Shunammite's son in a similar way. And John 6:35 shows Jesus as the true bread of life, of which the widow's jar was only a small foretaste.
basic.oneStory: Even while judging Israel's idolatry, the LORD extends His care to an outsider, a widow from the very region that gave Israel its queen and her false god. This anticipates the great turn of the whole Bible's story, that God's covenant purposes were always headed toward all nations, fulfilled in Christ, who feeds the hungry, welcomes outsiders, and raises the dead.

context.literary: The chapter unfolds in two matched scenes joined by a common theme, provision from resources that appear exhausted: first Elijah is fed by unclean birds at a drying brook, then a poor widow's flour and oil sustain three lives through famine without failing. The final scene intensifies the miracle from sustaining life to restoring it, and the narrator lets Elijah's own urgent prayers, not narrated action alone, carry the climax in verses 20 to 21.
context.historical: The drought Elijah announces directly confronts Baal, worshiped by Jezebel as the god of storm and fertility; that Elijah is sent to Sidon itself, Jezebel's own homeland, during the height of the famine his word caused, sharpens the contest further. Zarephath lay on the coast between Tyre and Sidon, entirely outside Israelite territory, so the widow who shelters Elijah is a Gentile, outside Israel's covenant, yet chosen by the LORD's own explicit command, verse 9.
context.canonical: This chapter opens what is often called the Elijah cycle, running through 2 Kings 2, in which the LORD contends for exclusive worship against Baal. The provision of bread that does not run out anticipates later feeding miracles, and Jesus Himself singles out this very widow in Luke 4:25-26 as proof that God has always reached beyond Israel to the nations.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that the LORD, not Baal, rules rain and famine, and that His prophet's word can be trusted absolutely, even when it requires living on ravens and a poor widow's dwindling supplies, and even in the face of a child's death.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is historical narrative recounting specific, unrepeatable miracles; it does not promise that every act of faith will produce a never emptying jar or a resurrection. What endures is the character it reveals: the LORD provides for those who trust His word, He hears urgent, wrestling prayer, and He is free to show mercy to whomever He chooses, including outsiders to the covenant.

debates.question: Why does the LORD send Elijah to a Gentile widow in Sidon rather than sustain him within Israel?

views.label: A rebuke and sign to unfaithful Israel
views.case: Jesus' own reading in Luke 4:25-26 stresses that there were many widows in Israel in Elijah's day, yet Elijah was sent to none of them but to a widow in Sidon; the choice itself indicts Israel's unbelief and previews grace going to outsiders.

views.label: Provision hidden precisely where Jezebel's power was strongest
views.case: Sending Elijah into Baal's own territory, Jezebel's homeland, to be fed there by the LORD's command underlines that the true God's rule extends even into the heart of the enemy's supposed domain.

debates.bearing: Either reading magnifies the LORD's sovereign freedom over Baal's territory and previews the gospel's reach beyond Israel to the nations, fulfilled in Christ.

debates.question: Was the widow's son actually dead, or merely gravely ill, in verse 17?

views.label: Truly dead
views.case: The text says plainly that no breath remained in him, and Elijah later prays for his life to return to him, verse 21, language that most naturally describes a genuine death and resurrection, matching the woman's own despair.

views.label: A severe, life threatening collapse
views.case: Some read the description more cautiously as life ebbing to its very last breath rather than certain clinical death, though the woman's grief and Elijah's prayer for life to return still describe a real, desperate crisis met by God's power.

debates.bearing: Either way the text presents an act of God's reviving power granted through urgent prayer, and the woman's own confession in verse 24 treats it as a decisive, faith-confirming act of God through His prophet.

study.originalLanguages: Elijah's opening oath concerns matar, rain, the very domain Baal claimed to rule as the Canaanite storm god; by shutting it up in the LORD's name, Elijah stakes everything on whose word truly governs the skies. The widow's desperate need is for lechem, bread, and the LORD's promise concerns her jug of shemen, oil, alongside her jar of flour, neither of which will fail until the day rain finally falls again. When her son dies, she cries out to Elijah as ish ha-elohim, man of God, first as an accusation born of grief and then, once the boy is alive again, as a settled confession that the word of the LORD in his mouth is emet, truth.

typology.type: Bread and oil that do not run out through famine
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the bread of life, whose provision for His people never fails, John 6:35, and who multiplies bread for the hungry crowds, Matthew 14:13-21.

typology.type: The prophet who raises a dead child by pleading urgently with the LORD
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who raises the dead by His own word and authority, needing no intercession to another, John 11:43-44, and who was Himself raised as the firstfruits of resurrection, 1 Corinthians 15:20.

typology.type: The LORD's care reaching a Gentile widow outside the covenant
typology.fulfillment: The gospel opened to the nations, which Jesus points to directly in Luke 4:25-26, and which Paul develops fully in Ephesians 2:11-13.

study.christ: Jesus Himself names this very widow in Luke 4:25-26 to prove that God's saving mercy was never confined to physical Israel, foreshadowing His own rejection at Nazareth and the gospel's later reach to the Gentiles. The jar and jug that do not run dry anticipate Christ, the bread of life, whose provision for His people never fails, John 6:35, and whose feeding of the five thousand echoes this same pattern of abundance from apparent scarcity. And Elijah's urgent, repeated prayer that restores breath to a dead child points forward to Christ, who raises the dead simply by His own word and authority, John 11:43-44, and who was Himself raised from death as the firstfruits of the resurrection to come, 1 Corinthians 15:20.
study.god: God is sovereign over nature, shutting and opening the heavens at His spoken word. He is a compassionate provider who sustains the humanly hopeless, a poor widow down to her very last meal, and He hears urgent, even wrestling prayer offered honestly in grief. He keeps His promises with striking precision, the jar and jug failing not once across the length of a famine, and He is free to extend His mercy to an outsider to the covenant even while judging Israel's idolatry in the surrounding chapters.

crossReferences.note: Jesus names this very widow to show that God's mercy in Elijah's day already reached beyond Israel to a Gentile in Sidon.
James holds up Elijah's prayer over the rain as the pattern for the ordinary believer's righteous, fervent prayer.
Elisha later raises the Shunammite's son in a strikingly similar scene, extending Elijah's pattern of resurrection through prayer.
Jesus declares Himself the bread of life, the far greater fulfillment of the jar of flour that did not run out.
The drought announced in 17:1 is finally lifted, tying this chapter directly into the following contest on Mount Carmel.

study.oneStory: Even while judging Israel's idolatry in the surrounding chapters, the LORD reaches out in tender care to an outsider, a widow from the very region that gave Israel its queen and her false god. This anticipates the great turn the whole Bible is making, that God's covenant purposes were always headed toward all nations, not Israel alone, a purpose fulfilled in Christ, who feeds the hungry, welcomes the outsider, and raises the dead by His own word.

sermon.title: A Widow's Joy
sermon.why: Preached at Redeemer Presbyterian Church in the series Discerning the Spirits (with Elijah), this sermon expounds 1 Kings 17:7-24, the widow's household sustained by never-failing flour and oil and her son's raising, closely matching this movement's central units on God's provision for the desperate outsider.

quiz.q: Why does Elijah announce a drought to Ahab at the start of the chapter?

options.text: To declare, in the LORD's name, that neither dew nor rain will fall except at His word, directly confronting Baal's claimed power over storm and fertility, v1.
options.note: This is exactly what Elijah's oath states and what it implies about the LORD versus Baal.

options.text: Because he was personally frustrated with Ahab's rule.
options.note: The text gives no such personal motive; Elijah speaks in the LORD's name about who truly controls the rain.

options.text: Because a drought had already begun and Elijah merely reported it.
options.note: The text reverses this: Elijah's word causes the drought to begin, rather than merely describing one already underway.

options.text: Because the LORD had promised abundant rain that year.
options.note: The opposite is announced: no rain until Elijah's word releases it.

quiz.q: How did the LORD provide for Elijah at the Brook of Cherith?

options.text: Ravens brought him bread and meat morning and evening, and he drank from the brook, vv 4-6.
options.note: This is exactly what the text describes.

options.text: Local villagers brought him food out of respect for his office.
options.note: The text specifies ravens, not villagers, as the means of provision.

options.text: He found abandoned stores of grain nearby.
options.note: This detail is invented and not found in the text.

options.text: He received no provision and nearly starved.
options.note: The opposite is true; he was fed reliably until the brook itself dried up.

quiz.q: What did the LORD promise the widow of Zarephath through Elijah?

options.text: That her jar of flour would not be exhausted and her jug of oil would not run dry until the day the LORD sent rain, v14.
options.note: This is the LORD's exact promise given through Elijah.

options.text: That she would receive a large harvest from her own field that season.
options.note: This detail is invented; the promise concerns her existing jar and jug, not a future harvest.

options.text: That Elijah would give her silver to buy more food in the city.
options.note: No such promise of silver appears in the text.

options.text: That she and her son would certainly die of the famine.
options.note: This is the opposite of the promise given, which secures their survival.

quiz.q: What did the widow accuse Elijah of when her son died, and how did Elijah respond, vv 18-22?

options.text: She thought Elijah had come to expose her sin and cause her son's death; Elijah took the boy, prayed urgently, and the LORD returned his life.
options.note: This matches both her accusation in verse 18 and Elijah's response in verses 19 to 22.

options.text: She blamed Elijah for the ongoing famine, and he apologized and left.
options.note: Her accusation concerns her son's death and her own iniquity, not the famine, and Elijah does not leave.

options.text: She asked Elijah to leave immediately, and he complied without response.
options.note: The opposite happens; Elijah takes the child and prays urgently rather than leaving.

options.text: She thanked Elijah for her son's continued good health.
options.note: The son had just died; this contradicts the scene entirely.

quiz.q: What did the widow conclude after her son was raised, v24?

options.text: That Elijah was truly a man of God and that the word of the LORD in his mouth was truth.
options.note: This is her exact confession in verse 24.

options.text: That Elijah personally possessed unusual power over life and death.
options.note: Her confession credits the word of the LORD through Elijah, not a personal power belonging to him alone.

options.text: That she should now worship Baal alongside the LORD.
options.note: This contradicts the entire point of the miracle, which vindicates the LORD alone.

options.text: That the miracle proved nothing about Elijah or the LORD.
options.note: The opposite is stated; she reaches full conviction because of what she has just witnessed.

questions: Elijah was fed first by ravens and then by a widow with almost nothing left. Where has God provided for you through unlikely or uncomfortable means you would not have chosen?
The widow was ready to prepare her very last meal and die, yet God asked her to give first before she received anything back. What is God asking you to trust Him with before you can see the outcome?

Elijah brought his honest anguish, even blaming questions, straight to God when the boy died. How comfortable are you bringing your rawest doubts and complaints directly to the LORD rather than hiding them?
It took a resurrection for the widow to fully trust that Elijah's word was the LORD's truth. What would it take for you to trust God's word more fully in an area where you still hold back?
Jesus later pointed to this very widow, an outsider, as proof of God's wide mercy. Who in your life might you be tempted to think is beyond the reach of God's grace?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote allows 'who was among the settlers of Gilead' to be rendered 'who was from Tishbe in Gilead,' identifying Elijah's hometown rather than merely his residence.
The BSB footnote allows 'the Brook of Cherith' to be rendered 'the Cherith Ravine,' a wadi east of the Jordan that would run dry in a prolonged drought, as it does in verse 7.
The source text's dash in 'I have no bread, only a handful of flour' has been rendered as a comma in this file; no dash characters are used in Makor's house style.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Kings 17:1-24. One em dash in verse 12, 'no bread, only a handful,' was converted to a comma; no other dashes appear in the source text for this chapter.
